feature: Words whitespace comments newlines and basic shell operators

Parameter expansion occurs inside double quotes while literal spaces remain part of the same argument in a representative case.

Inside double quotes, backslash preserves ordinary backslashes before non-special characters and quotes representative special characters such as dollar sign, backquote, and backslash.
Inside double quotes, a backslash before a non-special character remains literal along with that character in a representative case.

Inside double quotes, a backslash before a backquote preserves the backquote literally instead of starting legacy command substitution in a representative case.
Inside double quotes, a backslash before another backslash preserves one literal backslash in a representative case.

A backslash followed by newline is removed inside double quotes before token interpretation in a representative case.

Legacy backquote recognition covers basic substitution, escaped literal delimiters, escaped nested legacy substitutions, double-quote interaction including inner quotes and backslash-double-quote removal, nonspecial backslash preservation, line continuation, literal newlines, and unterminated diagnostics.

Inside legacy backquote command substitution, a backslash before a non-special character is preserved in a representative case.

feature: escaped double-quote inside quoted backquote substitution

feature: Alias substitution timing and reserved-word interaction

notes: Alias definitions made by alias, eval, or dot commands are not visible to later commands on the same physical read line, while the same aliases are visible when a subsequent line is read.
Command-position aliases recursively expand while active aliases do not recurse indefinitely.

feature: Alias values ending in blank keep the next word eligible for alias substitution

feature: Aliases defined by an earlier complete command affect later commands

CST has here_doc_body nodes and lowering extracts ordered bodies after the command line, including multiple bodies in redirection order, quoted delimiter literal bodies that are not shell-tokenized, unquoted body expansion coverage, backslash-newline continuation, and <<- tab stripping at true line starts.
A simple here-doc body is recognized up to its delimiter and supplied as command input in a representative case.

feature: here-doc body is not parsed as shell syntax
notes: Reserved words, operators, unmatched quotes, and substitution-looking text inside here-doc bodies are treated as data rather than parsed as shell syntax in representative cases.

feature: multiple here-doc bodies after a command line

A mixed quoted here-doc delimiter has quotes removed to find the terminating delimiter and marks the body as literal in a representative case.
A <<- here-doc strips leading tab characters from body and delimiter lines in a representative case.

feature: Simple command assignments arguments and redirections
notes: Assignment prefixes and redirections are lowered into execution IR.
Covers && || semicolon and newline sequencing.

Real pipe execution exists for external and mixed stages, including POSIX compound commands in first and non-first pipeline positions; non-last shell-implemented stages use isolated pipeline environments, while Rush preserves documented last-stage current-shell side effects.

A here-doc redirection attached to an if command provides standard input to the selected command in a representative case.
Here-doc redirections attached to if, while, for, and case commands provide standard input to the compound command body or subject expansion.

feature: Arithmetic expansion with variables assignments and operators
notes: Rush uses signed 64-bit POSIX arithmetic: C-style decimal/octal/hex constants and variable values must fit the signed range, signed overflow reports arithmetic overflow, and bitwise/shift operations expose two's-complement signed results. Invalid arithmetic variable values fail instead of silently coercing to zero; arithmetic expressions preprocess nested parameter, command, arithmetic, and legacy backquote substitutions before evaluation; representative backslash-newline, escaped-substitution-initiator, unmatched raw backquote, quote-byte, missing operand, malformed parenthesis, malformed conditional, malformed comma, zero divisor, overflow, short-circuit skips, and assignment-word arithmetic diagnostics have focused coverage.
Arithmetic expansion evaluates representative integer addition and multiplication with standard precedence.
Arithmetic expansion resolves shell variable values by name and treats unset variable names as zero in representative integer expressions.
Arithmetic assignment and compound assignment update shell variables and yield the assigned integer value in representative expressions.
Arithmetic expansion supports representative comparison, logical, bitwise, shift, conditional, and comma operators.

feature: arithmetic logical and conditional short-circuit evaluation
notes: Arithmetic logical AND, logical OR, and conditional operators do not evaluate unused operands, so skipped division by zero, signed overflow, and assignment side effects are not observed in representative cases.

feature: arithmetic expression quote and backslash handling
notes: Arithmetic expressions are treated like double-quoted text except double-quote bytes are not special in representative cases: backslash-newline is removed, nested legacy backquote substitution is expanded, escaped substitution initiators remain literal, and remaining quote bytes produce arithmetic diagnostics.

feature: Input output append and noclobber redirections

Shell-visible fd tracking handles common duplication/close cases, same-command external input/output fd duplication including direct non-stdio fd inheritance for simple commands, stdio duplication from non-stdio fds and shell-open fd targets, external fd close/restoration, external stdio close redirections and inherited stdio-to-stdio routing, pipeline stages, and async external commands, and representative bad input/output fd duplication errors.
Creates missing files and avoids truncating existing files.

feature: Here-doc expansion quoted delimiters and tab stripping
notes: Safe fd materialization, parser body nodes, expanded/unexpanded bodies with POSIX body quoting rules (quotes literal, backslash special only before dollar/backquote/backslash/newline, no tilde expansion), tab stripping, multiple here-docs, mixed quoted delimiter quote-removal including preserved non-special backslashes, and compound-command stdin here-docs are covered.
A simple here-doc redirection supplies its collected body as standard input to the command in a representative case.
An unquoted here-doc body expands parameter, command, and arithmetic substitutions before supplying command input in a representative case.
A quoted here-doc delimiter suppresses parameter, command, and arithmetic expansion in the body before supplying command input in a representative case.
Quote removal on a mixed quoted delimiter forms the delimiter token and disables here-doc body expansion.
Multiple here-doc redirections on one command collect their bodies in order, and the later stdin redirection supplies command input in a representative case.
A <<- here-doc strips leading tab characters from body and delimiter lines before supplying the resulting body as command input.

feature: Real fd redirections for builtins functions and groups
notes: Real inherited-stdio fd redirections cover builtins, functions, function definitions, brace groups, subshells, and if/for/while/until/case compounds, including arbitrary fd output and shared input offsets across mixed builtin/external consumers; captured-mode evidence covers synthetic command-not-found diagnostics and command-substitution cases.
Input redirection on a brace group supplies standard input to commands executed in the group.
A redirection attached to a builtin command captures its stdout in a file while later commands continue with normal stdout in a representative case.
A stderr redirection attached to a builtin command redirects the builtin diagnostic away from the shell stderr stream in a representative special-builtin failure case.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.7 Redirection; 2.9.5 Function Definition Command
notes: A redirection attached to a function invocation captures the function body stdout while later commands continue with normal stdout in a representative case.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.7 Redirection; 2.9.5 Function Definition Command
notes: A redirection attached to a function invocation supplies standard input to commands executed in the function body.
A redirection attached to a brace group captures grouped command stdout while later commands continue with normal stdout in a representative case.
A redirection attached to a subshell captures grouped command stdout while later commands continue with normal stdout in a representative case.
A redirection attached to a subshell supplies standard input to commands executed in the subshell.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.7 Redirection; 2.9.5 Function Definition Command
notes: Redirections attached to a function definition are saved and applied on each function invocation, including output truncation and input redirection.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.7 Redirection; 2.9.5 Function Definition Command
notes: An output redirection attached to a function definition is saved with the function and captures invocation stdout in a representative repeated-call case.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.7 Redirection; 2.9.5 Function Definition Command

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.7 Redirection; 2.9.4.4 If Conditional Construct

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.7 Redirection; 2.9.4.4 If Conditional Construct
notes: A redirection attached to an if compound command supplies stdin to the condition command in a representative case.
A redirection attached to a for compound command captures loop body stdout while later commands continue with normal stdout in a representative case.
A redirection attached to a while compound command captures loop body stdout while later commands continue with normal stdout in a representative case.
A redirection attached to a while compound command feeds stdin to repeated condition commands while loop body state remains in the current shell in a representative case.
A redirection attached to an until compound command captures loop body stdout while later commands continue with normal stdout in a representative case.

feature: if for while until compound redirections

notes: Unit coverage verifies a script-file operand is read as a whole file, $0 is the script path, remaining operands become positional parameters, shebang/comment first lines are comments, here-doc bodies survive chunked execution, and invocation shell options before the script apply.

notes: Interactive startup unit coverage verifies ENV is loaded only through interactive invocation paths, its value is parameter-expanded for $HOME and ${ENV_DIR} pathnames before sourcing, the file runs in the current shell environment, and it runs before the interactive command string.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.9.1.4 Command Search and Execution

feature: Initial environment import and assignment prefixes
notes: Environment import, startup IFS/PPID/PWD initialization, and command-prefix assignment semantics exist.

notes: export NAME marks an unset name so a later assignment is included in utility environments.

unset defaults to variable removal and supports explicit -v and -f modes.

notes: All 15 POSIX special builtins are classified; assignment-prefix persistence is covered across them while function assignment prefixes are temporary. XCU 2.8.1 failure consequences are split into the supported errors-special-builtin rows.
Implemented as special builtin.
eval executes an exit command in the current shell environment and terminates subsequent script execution.

Current-shell effects, PATH search for slashless operands without implicit current-directory fallback, return stopping the dot script with its status, empty-script status zero, syntax-error diagnostics stopping the non-interactive shell, and missing/non-readable operand diagnostics have corpus evidence.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU dot utility; XCU 2.8.1 Consequences of Shell Errors
notes: Missing file operands, nonexistent dot scripts, slashless operands absent from PATH, and non-readable dot scripts report diagnostics and stop non-interactive execution as special-builtin failures.

notes: Loop control works in loops, including nested and over-depth break/continue levels, status propagation after break/continue, outside-loop no-op behavior, and operand diagnostics with non-interactive shell-exit consequences.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU cd and pwd utilities
notes: cd, pwd, cd -L/-P, pwd -L/-P, CDPATH including slash operands, dot/dot-dot exclusion and dot-entry output, OLDPWD, logical dot-dot, -- option terminators, missing-directory diagnostics, and usage diagnostics have corpus/negative coverage.

Lookup modes, clustered -p/-v/-V options, multiple operands, -- option termination, alias/function/reserved-word/special-builtin/regular-builtin classification, status 127 lookup failures, -p default-path lookup without child PATH replacement, function lookup suppression, declaration-utility assignment expansion for wrapped export/readonly, and suppression of special-builtin assignment-prefix persistence and shell-exit error consequences have evidence.
command -p uses the default utility path for lookup without replacing PATH in the executed utility environment.
command -V reports shell builtins in the stable format shared by comparison shells.
command -v and command -V accept multiple command_name operands, report each found command, and return non-zero if any lookup fails.
command -v reports a shell function by name without executing it.
command executes the utility/builtin path rather than a shell function with the same name.
command accepts -- to terminate option parsing for execution and lookup modes.
Unsupported options and missing lookup operands report diagnostics and leave non-interactive execution running as regular builtin failures.

type is a regular shell builtin using the shell's own command lookup state, with representative coverage for aliases, reserved words, functions, regular and special builtins, PATH utilities, multiple operands, --, missing-name diagnostics, and not-found status.
Missing name operands report diagnostics and leave non-interactive execution running as regular builtin failures.

fc is a regular shell builtin wired to Rush interactive command history. Command numbers use sqlite history ids when sqlite history is attached and one-based in-memory insertion order otherwise; the triggering fc line is suppressed from interactive history while commands executed by fc are appended. POSIX corpus covers the builtin/no-history diagnostic path because the expected-output runner is non-interactive; executor and REPL tests cover attached-history listing and re-execution.

fc -l supports -n, -r, default previous-16 range, numeric absolute and negative relative references, string-prefix references, clamped ranges, and stable number-tab-command listing against attached interactive history.

fc -s supports old=new first-match substitution and first operands; editor mode supports -r, -e editor, and -e - no-edit execution. Resulting commands execute in the current shell, are appended to attached history, and the invoking fc command is not appended.
fc reports clear diagnostics for unavailable or empty history, invalid references, unresolved string references, unsupported options, missing editors, and unavailable history append/editor IO state.

hash is a regular shell builtin backed by the executor-owned PATH utility cache, with representative coverage for explicit utility operands, no-operand output, -r reset, PATH invalidation, missing utility diagnostics, and builtin/function exclusion.
hash without operands reports remembered external PATH utilities and produces no builtin or function entries.
hash -r clears remembered command locations in the current shell environment.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU hash utility; XCU 2.9.1 Simple Commands
notes: Changing PATH clears remembered utility pathnames before subsequent hash output or command lookup.
Missing utility operands produce a diagnostic and a non-zero regular-builtin status without clearing existing remembered external utilities.
Builtin and shell function operands are not recorded in the external utility cache or reported by no-operand hash output.

Environment printing, exported-only output, -i, -- before and after -i, assignment operands, command execution, PATH operand lookup, argument/status propagation, invalid-option, command-not-found, and permission-denied diagnostics have corpus coverage.
env accepts -- both after -i and before assignment/command operands.

feature: env option terminator before assignment command
notes: env accepts -- before assignment operands and runs the command with that temporary environment.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU env utility; XCU 2.9.1 Simple Commands
notes: env output includes exported variables and omits unexported shell-local variables.
Unsupported options report diagnostics, and missing env utility operands propagate command-not-found status while leaving non-interactive execution running.

Eval concatenates operands, handles empty input, executes in the current shell including assignment, exit, and redirected stdin/read side effects, and has syntax, expansion, and redirection failure consequence coverage.

feature: eval argument concatenation and empty status
notes: eval concatenates arguments for execution and empty eval returns success.

Real replacement exists for safe inherited external command path, failed replacement exits non-interactive shell execution, permission-denied replacement reports status 126, no-return function context is covered, and redirection-only exec applies fd changes to the current shell.
exec without command operands applies redirections to the current shell for stdout save/redirect/restore and arbitrary input fd duplication flows.
Explicit status behavior, low-8-bit status truncation, default previous-status behavior, function-body shell exit, and usage diagnostics are covered.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU return utility; XCU 2.8.1 Consequences of Shell Errors
notes: Non-interactive return outside a function, invalid numeric operands, and excess operands diagnose and stop execution with status 2.
exit without an operand exits with the previous command status.
return uses its explicit numeric operand or the previous command status when no operand is supplied.

feature: readonly marks names against later assignment
notes: readonly NAME prevents a later assignment-only command from modifying the variable and exits non-interactive execution with a diagnostic.
readonly -p lists names marked readonly before assignment without inventing an assigned value.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU export and readonly utilities
notes: export -p and readonly -p expose marked variables in a reusable listing form for simple values, including readonly names without assigned values.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU export and readonly utilities
notes: export -p and readonly -p quote assigned values with shell single-quote syntax for representative values containing spaces.

Clusters, separate and attached option-arguments, silent and non-silent missing/invalid option handling, explicit arg operands, -- termination, OPTIND initialization and reset, validation diagnostics, and readonly output variable failures are covered.

notes: unalias accepts -- as an option terminator before alias-name operands.

Missing format operands, including printf --, malformed formats, unsupported conversions, and invalid numeric operands report diagnostics and leave non-interactive execution running as regular builtin failures.

Backslash, continuation including interactive terminal PS2 prompting, EOF trailing backslash, -r, -- option termination including after -r, custom/mixed/empty IFS, last-variable assignment, EOF status, and usage diagnostics are covered.

POSIX-defined test and [ behavior is covered for missing expressions, one-argument string truthiness, leading ! two-, three-, and four-argument forms, unary file and string primaries, string and integer binary primaries, file comparisons, bracket close diagnostics, and representative invalid-expression status; Issue 8 removed historical -a/-o/parentheses expression grammar from the POSIX-required surface, so retained compatibility coverage is tracked outside this POSIX claim.

Produces the required two POSIX-style user/system CPU-time lines for the shell and children using getrusage when libc is available, with centisecond formatting covered by unit tests and runtime-dependent values validated by corpus shape checks. Operand diagnostics are split separately.
Excess operands report diagnostics and exit non-interactive execution as a special-builtin failure.

feature: Trap listing EXIT and common signal traps
notes: Trap utility behavior covers reusable listing output, reset and null-ignore actions, -- option termination, invalid signal diagnostics, EXIT trap execution/status preservation, common catchable signal dispatch, trap-action exit behavior, and caught-trap reset in subshells and command substitutions; real signal dispatch details are covered by signal-trap-real.
trap accepts -- before action operands and as a no-action listing terminator.
An EXIT trap observes the shell status that caused shell termination.
Invalid signal specifications are diagnosed and exit non-interactive execution as a special-builtin failure.

Basic get/set behavior, current-shell plus subshell/pipeline mask effects, -S symbolic output, symbolic mask operands including chmod-style multiple actions and permission copies, comma-separated assignment clauses, and -- option terminator behavior are covered; diagnostics are split separately.
Symbolic umask operands cover u/g/o/a, r/w/x/X, +, -, =, representative comma-separated assignment clauses, multiple actions per clause, and permission-copy operands.
umask accepts representative comma-separated symbolic assignment clauses and computes the expected mask.
Invalid numeric syntax and symbolic masks, operands after -- that look like options, unsupported options, and excess operands report diagnostics while allowing subsequent commands to run.

Tracked background pids and jobs are waitable; wait with no operands waits all known jobs and returns zero unless interrupted by a trapped signal; pid/job operands return the last operand status; current, previous, numeric, prefix, and substring job ID operands are accepted where unambiguous; -- is accepted as an option terminator; stopped and trapped-signal wait behavior is covered by adjacent job-control and signal rows.
wait accepts -- as an option terminator before optional pid operands.
Invalid pid operands, unknown pids, and jobs removed after their status report use Rush's regular builtin failure status 127, report diagnostics, and leave non-interactive execution running.
wait accepts current, previous, numeric, prefix, and substring job-control job ID operands and returns the waited job status where the match is unambiguous.

notes: kill is a shell builtin for POSIX job ID operands, accepts TERM by default plus -s signal, -signal, -0, pid operands, and current/previous/numeric/prefix/substring job IDs, sending to a tracked job process group when available while preserving ordinary pid signaling.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU alias and unalias utilities
notes: Alias/unalias utility behavior is covered independently of alias token timing: POSIX name=value listing/query, reusable value quoting, POSIX alias-name operands, current-shell alias persistence/removal, unalias -a, and unalias -- operands. Exact substitution timing remains tracked by lex-alias-token-timing.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU alias and unalias utilities
notes: Invalid alias names, missing unalias operands, unsupported unalias options, and missing alias queries report diagnostics and leave non-interactive execution running as regular builtin failures.

External background jobs spawn real children and inherit command redirections, including stdout/stderr duplication routing.

Forked builtin, brace, subshell, loop, and pipeline async jobs are waitable, set $! in the parent, preserve parent job state across nested subshell async, inherit shell options while resetting caught traps for the async subshell environment, use /dev/null for default stdin when job control is disabled, report compound redirection diagnostics, and share the supported process-group/stopped-state/notification behavior covered by adjacent job-control rows.

notes: Unit and guarded PTY coverage verify foreground external-only pipelines share a process group in interactive inherited-stdio paths, foreground inherited-stdio mixed pipelines fork through a job-owned wrapper process group before terminal handoff, stopped foreground mixed pipelines restore the shell foreground process group and resume after SIGTSTP/SIGTTIN/SIGTTOU stops, set -m/monitor puts tracked async external and forked compound/mixed-pipeline jobs in their own process groups in interactive and non-interactive shells, background monitor jobs do not take foreground terminal ownership, and non-interactive default async jobs remain in the shell process group.

notes: Monitor-enabled fg writes the command, waits for current/explicit jobs, returns the job status, hands the terminal to saved process groups, sends SIGCONT to stopped jobs, and removes completed foreground jobs from the waitable table; bg supports current/previous/numeric/prefix/substring and multiple operands with POSIX [%d] %s output and reports disabled job control as an error.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU 2.11 Signals and Error Handling; XCU job control utilities

notes: Editor, signal-source, and signal-disposition tests cover Ctrl-C/SIGINT line discard to a fresh prompt with `$?` preserved by the REPL loop, INT traps running from the interactive interrupt path, interactive shell INT/QUIT/TERM handlers, and foreground job child/wrapper reset to default INT/QUIT/TERM dispositions. notes: Atomic pending-signal dispatch for common signals between commands, signal trap exit, EXIT trap status preservation, ignored-at-entry non-interactive signal behavior, caught signal trap reset in subshells and command substitutions, trap action status preservation, EXIT trap ordering for signal and errexit exits, wait interruption status for trapped signals, reusable trap listing quoting, and prompt editor wake/redraw for process-directed trapped signals are covered.

notes: set supports -o/+o option listing, POSIX short flags -abefmnuvxC, set -- positional operands, option parsing followed by positional operands, set -/+ operand terminators, set - to disable xtrace/verbose, no-operand shell variable listing, clustered short option toggles, nounset/noglob/noclobber/noexec/ignoreeof/vi, Rush's implementation-extension emacs option, short/long allexport, monitor/-m and notify/-b option state with $- reflection, monitor-mode process groups for tracked interactive async jobs, interactive notify polling while the editor is active, vi command-line editing mode for the terminal-independent editor, and no-effect compatibility for obsolescent -h and nolog.

notes: Ordinary shell variable assignments are not exported by default, while set -a and set -o allexport mark subsequent assignments for export until disabled.

notes: POSIX.1-2024 omits the historical shell emacs command-line editing mode and leaves non-vi editing modes as implementation extensions. Rush exposes its default terminal-independent Emacs-style editor as set -o emacs: it is on by default, set -o emacs disables vi, set -o vi disables emacs, set +o emacs clears the explicit emacs option state without enabling vi, and set +o vi restores Rush's default Emacs-style editing option. Unit coverage backs representative non-modal control/meta bindings including Ctrl-A/E/B/F/K/U/W/Y/T/P/N/D/H and Meta-B/F/D/backspace.

notes: set -o vi and set +o vi toggle Rush's interactive editing mode state; the terminal-independent editor covers vi insert, command, and replace modes, representative motion/change/delete/yank/put/undo/history commands, POSIX / ? n N history pattern search, previous-line bigword insertion with _, @ command aliases from enabled _letter shell aliases processed as editing input, [number]v external-editor requests for the current or numbered history command, dot-repeat for prior insert sessions including backspace/word erase/cursor movement controls, replace-mode R sessions, single-character replace/delete/put/toggle/operator changes, counted input repetition, multiplicative operator/motion counts, out-of-range no-change operator motions, change-word blank handling, and pathname editing commands = \\ * on the current shell bigword including implicit *, sorted matches, directory marks, quoted/backslash-escaped glob literals, tilde/parameter/command/arithmetic expansion with unquoted generated field splitting before matching, and single-quote/double-quote/backslash replacement preservation for quoted input, and renders beam, block, and underline cursor shapes for insert, command, and replace modes respectively. Remaining POSIX vi command-line editing edge commands outside this slice are tracked as follow-up work.

posix_ref: POSIX.1-2024 XCU set utility; XCU 2.8.1 Consequences of Shell Errors
notes: Unsupported short options and unknown -o option names report diagnostics and stop non-interactive execution as special-builtin failures.

Covers -e enable/disable, simple failures, conditional command suppression, AND-OR and negated-pipeline suppression, final AND-OR failures, assignment command-substitution status, individual pipeline and command-substitution subshell failures, non-subshell compound command ignored-status exceptions, and function/subshell boundary cases.

feature: errexit enable, disable, and simple command failures
notes: set -e exits on a representative failing simple command, and set +e disables that behavior.

feature: errexit pipeline, command-substitution, and subshell environments
notes: Individual failures before the last pipeline command and inside command-substitution subshells do not exit the parent shell when the containing pipeline or command succeeds; failing pipeline status still exits.
Non-subshell compound commands do not trigger errexit when their non-zero status was produced while -e was ignored; subshell commands and function calls remain boundary cases that do trigger errexit.
Unset parameter diagnostics, disabling nounset, and default-operator suppression are covered for representative non-interactive cases.

Current-shell expansion failures exit non-interactive execution; command-substitution failures exit only the substitution subshell while surfacing diagnostics/status; unit coverage verifies interactive expansion failures abort the current command without exiting the prompt loop.
